title: Arbitrary manipulation of nuclear spins in hexagonal boron nitride
abstract: Due to its localized nature and controllability, the negatively charged boron vacancy centers (V$_\text{B}^-$) in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) are a promising spin platform for accessing its neighboring nuclei with potential for performing quantum computational tasks. However, the methods of utilizing and manipulating the nuclear spins are still lacking. In this work, we propose a protocol for the preparation of single- and multi-qubit gates on the nuclear spins, utilizing the electron spin as an auxiliary qubit. By applying a background magnetic field and a multi-tone continuous drive, we show that the electron spin coupling to the nuclei can be efficiently engineered. This allows for suppressing the undesired electron-nuclear interactions through the Hahn echo pulse sequence. The target gates are then implemented by employing proper RF drives. Our numerical results for realistic parameters show gate fidelities as high as $99\%$ for single-qubit and $95\%$ for multi-qubit gates. With the gate execution durations being less than $300$ ns, our protocol evades electron spin decoherence effects. Therefore, our scheme sets the stage for the practical application of V$_\text{B}^-$ in hBN for quantum computation purposes.
summary: This paper proposes a detailed quantum control protocol for manipulating nuclear spins in boron vacancy centers within hBN. By leveraging the electron spin as a mediator, the authors engineer controlled interactions to implement high-fidelity single- and multi-qubit gates. The results demonstrate that the scheme is fast enough to evade electron spin decoherence, paving the way for quantum computation in this solid-state platform.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a robust protocol for implementing arbitrary single- and multi-qubit quantum gates on the nuclear spins within a hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) platform.
main_result: A protocol was established that achieves high gate fidelities (up to 99% for single-qubit gates) with short gate execution times (< 300 ns), enabling practical quantum computation using boron vacancy centers.
method: The protocol utilizes the electron spin as an auxiliary qubit, employing multi-tone continuous drives and Hahn echo pulse sequences to engineer and control the electron-nuclear coupling.
model_or_system: The system is based on negatively charged boron vacancy centers ($	ext{V}_	ext{B}^-$) in hBN, modeled by a total Hamiltonian involving electron ($\hat{H}_e$), nuclear ($\hat{H}_n$), and interaction ($\hat{H}_i$) terms. The dynamics are analyzed by reducing the system to an effective two-level electron subspace.
key_observables: Gate fidelities (up to 99% single-qubit, 95% multi-qubit), gate execution duration (< 300 ns), and the coherence function $C_{WT}(\omega)$ for spin distinction.
important_parameters: Moderate background magnetic field ($B \sim 0.1-1 	ext{ T}$), and the decoherence rate ($\Gamma$) which must be overcome by fast gate operations.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the ability to suppress undesired electron-nuclear interactions using Hahn echo sequences and relies on the electron spin being fast enough to execute gates before decoherence.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spin cluster geometry, the dependence of gate infidelity on time and decoherence rate, and the coherence function showing distinct peaks for individual spin addressing.
paper_structure: The work progresses by first defining the Hamiltonian and the physical system, then detailing the gate implementation protocol using RF drives and Hahn echo sequences, and finally presenting numerical results demonstrating high fidelity and fast operation times.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the challenge of scalable quantum memory and gate operations in solid-state systems, which is a major focus area for quantum information scientists.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. (a) Fidelity of gate X1 under dephasing as a function of control period τ and evolution time t, with the cyan dot indicating the global maximum. (b) Fidelity of X1 and Y1 with (dashed) and without (solid) dephasing at the optimal point. The inset shows X1 fidelity dynamics for optimal τ. In (c) and (d), the infidelity profiles 1 −F for various single- and multi-qubit gates are plotted versus evolution time for optimized τ. The black dash-dotted lines in (b) to (d) indicate the infidelity of the identity gate as a reference.
FIG. 3. The infidelity 1 −F of the single-qubit conditional gates (a) CX1, CX2, CH1, and CT1, (b) the two-qubit gates CX1X2 and CX2X3, and the three-qubit gates CXXX and Hadamard gate CHHH.

FIG. 4. A consideration on the cluster of an electron spin (red ball) residing at the vacancy center and three nearest spins (green balls N1 to N3) embedded in VB-hB15N, is en- abled by their strong interactions relative to other terms. An adequately strong magnetic field B is applied in-plane at the angle ϕ from the dangling bond−2 connecting the central spin and the nuclear spin N2, where the effective fields ak are given by the blue arrows. Likewise, θk denotes the angles from the magnetic field direction and the dangling bond−k. For the coordinate of the electron spin operator, we define an axis ˆz in the direction of the magnetic field, whereas we define spin operators in terms of...

FIG. 5. The distributions of the ratio ωn/ak for various magnetic field strengths B and its orientation ϕ for nuclear spins N1 to N3. Here one can see the equivalence among the three spins, e.g., the translation of the angle ϕ by π/6 will result in a permutation of the spin labels k. The cyan dashed lines and dots indicate the points corresponding to the B = 250 mT and ϕ = 9π/2 used in the example in our calculation.

FIG. 6. The characteristics of the coherence function in the frequency domain c WT (ω) with T ∼1.6 µs in its normalized unit. For a fixed magnetic field strength B = 250 mT and the coherence rate Γ = 0.5 MHz, we can see the distinct peaks (a) for various orientations ϕ except at the points where ϕ is a multiple of π/6, i.e., the magnetic field is parallel or perpendicular to one of the dangling bonds. The distance between two adjacent peaks is larger for the larger magnetic field strength for a fixed angle ϕ = 9π/2 (b), given the coherence rate Γ = 0.5 MHz, reflecting that the strong magnetic field provides more robust separation between two nuclear spins.
